---
title: "Individual assignment 4"
date: 2026-05-01
---

# Set up

1. Create a new .qmd in the `code` folder of your `ENVS-193DD_week-04` folder. Make sure that you:
    - fill in your name in the "Author" field
    - are rendering to .pdf
    - leave the visual editor box unchecked
2. In the YAML of your .qmd, enter the date.
3. Create a set up section for your code (refer to class code if you need help with this).
4. Insert a code chunk to read in packages. 
    - At minimum, you will need `tidyverse`, `janitor`, and `here`.
5. Insert a code chunk to read in data.
    - Be sure that you are using `here()` along with `read_csv()`.
6. Insert a code chunk for class code that you need for your assignment.

:::{.callout-note title="Practice your own editorial judgement!" collapse="true"}
You will not need _all_ the code from class to do your assignment.  

You will need to pick and choose the components that are relevant to the assignment.  

If you copy-paste your entire class script into your homework, you will need to revise your assignment!
:::

7. Render to .pdf to make sure that you can render.
8. Start your assignment!

# Problems

## 1. Pool differences in mean percent cover

In this section, you will visualize differences in Coyote-thistle (_Eryngium vaseyi_) between pools and compare insights from your new visualization with the plot we made in class.

### a. Plan your figure

In part b, you will create a figure examining percent cover of Coyote-thistle across pools.

List the columns you would need from `filtered_explicit0` to create a figure matching this description.

- x-axis: 
- y-axis:
- geometry to represent each pool:
- geometry to represent mean mean percent cover (consider the mean of a mean):

:::{.callout-note title="Stuck?" collapse="true"}
We have made a plot like this before. Go back to the materials on water quality to see how we created a figure to compare mean salinity across the three sampling sites.
:::

### b. Code your figure

Code your figure following the list you outlined in part 1a. For full marks:

- make sure the x- and y-axes are labelled in a readable format
- include a title that:
    - describes the information shown in your figure and
    - specifies the range of years or time period for this data
- represent the mean in a way that is visually distinct from each individual pool with color, shape size, and shape type
- use a theme that is different from the ggplot() default

### c. Interpret your figure

In 1-2 sentences each, explain:

- which 2 pools have the most Coyote-thistle percent cover (on average), and which 2 pools have the least
- how the insights from this visualization differ and/or are similar to the visualization we made in class
- how pools differ in the spread of mean percent cover of Coyote-thistle

Be specific about the visual components that you're using to support your claim for each point.


## 2. Trends through time in native species richness

Restorationists are typically interested in species richness of native plant species.

In this section, you will calculate and visualize species richness at all vernal pools.

### a. Pseudocode for native species

The `pools_explicit0` data frame contains all plant species - native and non-native - that occur in vernal pools. 

Before visualizing native species richness, you will need to calculate it.

Before calculating species richness, you will need to have a list of native species with which to filter the `pools_explicit0` data frame.

In this section, write pseudocode to get an object called `native_spp` that is a _vector_ containing all unique values for native species from the `veg` data frame.

### b. Code your pseudocode

Code the pseudocode that you outlined in part a. 

At the end of your code chunk, use `sample()` to display 10 random entries from your vector.


### c. Calculate native species richness

Create a new data frame (call it whatever you want) in which you calculate species richness for each pool in a given year.

You should end up with a data frame that looks like this:

![](/assignments/images/individual-assignment-04/2c-example.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"}

At the end of your code chunk, use `slice_sample()` to display 10 random rows from your data frame.

:::{.callout-note title="Careful with your filtering steps!" collapse="true"}
This is an exercise in being careful about your species richness calculations. Are you calculating the total number of species that _could_ occur at a given pool in a given year, or are you calculating the total number of species that are _present_ at a given pool in a given year?
:::

:::{.callout-note title="To roll up or not" collapse="true"}
Note that you _will not_ need to roll up any subspecies. Happy to explain why if you are curious, but in short you are fine to calculate species richness without adjusting for subspecies identifications.
:::

### d. Code your figure

Create a timeseries figure depicting species richness through time faceted by pool. For full credit:

- color by pool
- use a color palette package that is _not_ `NatParksPalettes` or `colorbrewer` (google "R color palettes" for inspiration)
- use a different theme from the default
- take out the legend
- label the x- and y-axes


### e. Interpret your figure

In 1-2 sentences, describe:

- the pools in which species richness tends to be _increasing_, on average
- the pools in which species richness tends to be _decreasing_, on average
- the pools in which species richness tends to be _the same_, on average

Be specific about the visual components of the plot that you are using to make these claims.

## 3. Trends through time in native species percent cover

We spent a lot of time thinking about how to use `complete()` because we wanted to calculate the mean percent cover per species per pool per year, and doing so required handling implicit missingness.

However, there are calculations that _do not_ need adjustments for implicit missingness.

In this section, you will calculate and visualize the _total_ native species percent cover divided by the number of quadrats for each pool in a given year.

### a. Plan your figure

In part c, you will create a figure examining total native species percent cover through time. You will also facet your figure by pool.

List the columns you would need from a data frame _and the values they should contain_ to create a figure matching this description.

- x-axis: 
- y-axis:
- geometries (there are two):
- facet function and argument:

### b. Wrangle your data

Create an object called `native_pc` from `veg`. In no particular order (and this is not an exhaustive list),

- filter to only include native species
- filter to only include surveys from vernal pools at NCOS
- calculate the total native percent cover for a single pool in a year
- join with the `metadata` data frame
- calculate total native percent cover divided by the number of quadrats

At the end of your code chunk, use `slice_sample()` to display 10 random rows from `native_pc`.

### c. Code your figure

Code the figure you described in part a with the data frame you created in part b. For full credit:

- color by pool
- use the same colors as you did in 2d (for consistency)
- use a different theme from the default
- take out the legend
- label the x- and y-axes

### d. Interpret your figure

In 1-3 sentences each, explain:

- why it is possible to have y-axis values greater than 100%
- how trends through time in percent cover differ (if at all) from trends through time in species richness
- how this figure relates to Figure 8 in the monitoring report

Be specific about the visual components of the plot that you are using to make these claims.

# Assignment checklist

Your rendered PDF should:

- [ ] include your name, title, and submission date
- [ ] include all code with annotations (see "General formatting components" for details)   
- [ ] be organized and readable (see "General formatting components" for details)     
- [ ] be free of messages and warnings from code chunk evaluation

Your responses should include:  

for Problem 1:   

- [ ] written response for Problem 1a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 1b
- [ ] written response for Problem 1c

for Problem 2:  

- [ ] written response for Problem 2a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 2b
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 2c
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 2d
- [ ] written response for Problem 2e

for Problem 3:  

- [ ] written response for Problem 3a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 3b
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 3c
- [ ] written response for Problem 3d

Lastly, check out the **rubric on Canvas** to see the component breakdown in more detail.  

Your final submission should include:  

- [ ] a single PDF rendered from a .qmd file
- [ ] your submitted [Generative AI statement of use](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSevf1zSjndFN-XMyKrCmpiIl-K6pwP4aUShOzI94t0ZW-yiuw/viewform) by the due date

# General formatting components

You will only receive full marks for annotations if you have:  

- meaningful comments on each line of visualization code and/or ggplot geom/theme call (not needed for each argument, though good to have)  
- meaningful comments on each function in any piping operations  
- meaningful comments in set up code to denote where packages and/or data are read in

You will only receive full marks for readability if:  

- code includes carriage returns, spaces, etc. to make pipe operations and arguments clear  
- code includes consistent spacing and indents
- headers are used correctly throughout document
- text is formatted correctly (for example, superscripts/subscripts appear correctly)
- text is distinct from in-line code or references to functions/objects (e.g. using backticks to denote functions)
- bullet point lists appear as expected
